item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: In the absence of any other sources, and the specificity of the image, the image is assumed to be user-created and thus cc-compliant.

item.title: How did you do it
item.content:encoded: I'm planning on making my own SCP similar to this. I was wondering how you generated this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The phrases 'IT' and 'NO' are too short for Foundation statisticians to think them anomalous, unless they appeared far more often. Phrases with the same length as 'MAKE' and 'STOP' would appear almost 2000 times less often than 'IT' and 'NO'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the purposes of the SCP Board Game, Escape from site 19, we have made a photograph of SCP-1214. The author can legally use it per his discretion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That could be kind of cool if anything came of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh god.

…

I cannot look at a computer the same way again. This REALLY disturbed me for some reason. And the Foundation keeps running it *shudders* upvote and upvote again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a complete sucker for this one, the impossibility of this is just disturbing and ominous, awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was actually kind of afraid to open the outputs. Even though I knew there'd be only text in them.
This is my first post in… first post in general, never had one before on this site, and it's about me liking this.
Be proud.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the mystery so much in this one. +1
I'm also a programmer, so I can like articles of this kind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was amazing. Hm…. I want to feed this into a text to speech program now. I'm a sucker for computers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was definitely my favorite part. I don't know if it even need the example of retaliation on the good doctor; it might be eerier to just leave it be with that final message.

For example, you could picture it as aliens/whatever testing to see how humanity would react to slight evidence we were causing pain to the point of causing incapacitation. The Foundation kept testing, so I don't think humanity passed that little test. Now something has the signal locked and they're coming to kick our butts before we get our hands on more torture devices. Cliche? Probably. But still ominous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. It was alright.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have you tried turning it off and on again?

):
All I can say is ):

also +1 for irrational sympathy for a computer program.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it! I got where you were going with it, and it sufficiently creeped me out. Good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like. I'm a sucker for good computer-based anomalies as it is, but I particularly like how spare and bleak the vibe is that I get from this one.

Plus, all through the thing I'm thinking it's about a single, local strangeness, and then I read the "We respond in kind" part and I'm left wondering if there aren't multiple entities involved, and if this thing isn't more of a conduit to somewhere else than simply a self-contained, suffering AI. Basically, when I finished reading, my brain is going "What if [X]? But no, maybe [Y]??", and I really dig that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're probably right about it needing a bit of a trim. I found it quite difficult to balance "saying to little" and "saying too much". The first draft was very vague about what's really going on, and then I added more details for each rewrite.

Then again, I've seen some advice to cut out bits I consider vital. I guess finding that balance is something that gets at least a little bit easier with more experience.

As for your interpretation:

+ show block
– hide block

You're not far off the mark :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The best place to hide hidden bytes of code is in the compiler itself. Look up the Ken Thompson hack for details of how no computer in existence can ever be truly secure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just, wonderful. The mystery here is perfect.You make me question "What the hell is it" with a rather simple subject matter, a good old PRNG.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't bad at all, but I was kind of waiting for phase 3 to be the really creepy stuff, and it wasn't. The explanation makes sense, but doesn't come through, and I agree that it's non-anomalous until the addendum. Still, not a bad start, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's odd, I read it as somehow dragging a consciousness from another dimension into contact with the program, which then proceeded to randomly fry their mind. The final stinger would be that world's version of a Foundation attempting to fire back an attack to tell us to knock it off.

I haven't read the spoiler on the previous page though, so I have no idea if that's the author's intent.

I liked the piece, but as with my own attempts at writing it felt like it could really use another pass to cut down on needless things and trim it up a bit. I'll have to read it again later and see how it strikes me before I cast a vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, I'm glad you liked it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got it from here,

http://www.scp-wiki.net/security-clearance-levels

but actually, now that I read it again, I see I got it it wrong.

I thought 1/173-44 for instance, was person nr 44 with level 1 access to SCP-173, but I see now that it refers just to the security level itself, not to the person. My bad. I have changed that now. A few less numbers, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, I liked this one. I remember when it was posted once before and I'm glad you persisted with it.
The way I interpret it, somehow this random number generator is achieving sentience. An awful, existentially bleak sentience.
Its cries of dismay, its pleading puts me in the mind of desperate prayers. It unsettled me.

Thanks for submitting it =D
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The thing is, no matter how much 'there is no canon' people will slap onto you, there will always be a standard of how SCP articles are written. I would say the vast majority of articles refer to researchers as their researcher name like Assistant Researcher/Junior Researcher ClearlyIncompetent , Dr. Fuckwit, or Head Researcher NoEmpathy or something. While I have no problems to the other numerical terms labeled here(I've seen it done before and I have done it before without issue), the fact you basically did to researchers what most people do to d-class really confuses me, since I haven't seen such a thing happen before and don't know where you got that idea that it would be more tonally accurate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks :) It is my first, and I was very nervous posting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. I am a sucker for computer-related SCPs, I appreciated the pictures and the logs, too; the procedure in of itself, with the random "Please, no, stop" words would be interesting enough. The end seemed somewhat hurried (why the loss of the senses); I expected an incident, I actually really wanted to open some kind of a "Level 4 access only" log with a cryptic message in the text, but not the damage to the researcher, I believe that was unneeded.

Also, the single character passages like "aaaaaaaaaa" seemed really… disturbing.

This is the first SCP I see from you, Carnage, and it seems people quite like the SCP, so keep it up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the comments. I felt it was the clinical way to do it, referring to numbers instead of names or items, but I may have overdone it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm okay with the screaming (I totally understood that, for some reason it seemed according to other comment that was less obvious, so maybe I was just imagining I did), especially with the noted 'aaaaaaaa' sequences(although now that I think of it, it's probably only because of obscure japanese video games I played). Now, I saw where you were going UNTIL the addendum, of which I felt that you brought a whole 'ooooo daaaanger' thing for the sake of it and didn't really like it. However it is true that without it, the scp could be brushed off as completely nonanomalous. I was however rather confused as the excessive numbering of things and didn't think any of that was at all necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah. Frankly, by the time I got to that addendum, I was skimming as the rest of the article to that point wasn't grabbing me. I personally would've put that earlier in the description, but that's your stylistic choice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, upvoted.

Cue the "computers don't work that way!" crowd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It states in the article:
"SCP-1214 does not appear to be able to infect other computers, and copies of the SCP-1214 program will not exhibit anomalous properties."

No modifications has been made to the anomalous program as it is unknown if that would destroy the effect. Contain, Don't Destroy and all that.

I did read 603 after someone commented on it after my 2nd draft, but I personally thought my thing was different enough. YMMV.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm…maybe.

But the analysis of what the code does in the first part of the article, was intended to be correct. I assume the Foundation has people able to analyze code, and determine whether it does something unexpected or not.

As I said in another post…I originally intended to include the actual C# code in the article, but decided against it. Probably not that many readers that would find it interesting…but it would have proven that there is nothing hidden to explain the phenomenon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, the next point is, if the code itself is so non-anomalous, is this effect localized to the program itself, or to the laptop? I.e. if we ran the exact same code on a different computer, would the effects be the same? What happens if we run multiple instances simultaneously, on different partitions of the same laptop? What happens if we change the code slightly?

Again, as it stands, this reminds me far too much of SCP-603 and "Jacob"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It wasn't my intention that it should be humorous, at least not primarily. Wild, unsubstantiated rumors based on the SCP was what I was going for. Students exaggerating on purpose perhaps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Improbable, not impossible. It could still be sixty lines of deliriously complex homebrewed code a genius put together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The point is (and I tried to make that clear early) is that there is no weird code IN there that explains the behavior. There are 60 lines of code that should generate the characters from Table 1, and to consistently generate words of distress is insanely improbable.

I work as a software developer though, so I may not have made things as clear as I thought I did. That's on me.
And if it's not as interesting as I thought…well…that's one me too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it takes until the addendum to actually confirm that the thing isn't just some joke program some college guy made for kicks and giggles, (and even then not really prove that it isn't) I think it might not be anomalous enough

Gotta agree with this. Everything up to the addendum could be explained as a weird-ass, but non-anomalous, program. Your zinger that explains WHY this is anomalous shouldn't a) be buried as the absolute last thing on the page, or b) be hidden behind a collapsible.

As it is, this whole thing reminds me way too much of "Jacob" from that self-editing program thingie SCP. EDIT: SCP-603

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmm yeah, but such a thing would not be so easy to hide in 60 lines of code :)

I considered to include the actual code in the, but that would have been even more clutter…and perhaps not so interesting.

(Yeah I actually wrote the program that generated the random output - it didn't talk much to me though, I admit…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first half of the acquisition log seems like it was written to be humorous.
It doesn't work with the rest of the article.

*as a joke you should have made it shell script instead of C#

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*steeples fingers*

Quite…

(The "we respond in kind" bit was what got my upvote)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it takes until the addendum to actually confirm that the thing isn't just some joke program some college guy made for kicks and giggles, (and even then not really prove that it isn't) I think it might not be anomalous enough.

While my computer knowledge generally boils down to "kick it and/or recite litanies to the machine spirits", I think it would be conceivable that the long strings of letters and repeated words at certain points of time could just be programmed in, and not anomalous at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And I'll consider the rest of what you said as well - right now I'm exhausted by actually posting something live. It's quite a trial by fire around here, but that's what makes the site good, so if I burn, so be it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eeeh. I can see that, but the article overall still doesn't appeal to me personally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can explain it. It is sort of a spoiler, if that sort of thing counts here, so I'll hide it:

+ show block
– hide block

The long strings of the same characters are the entity "screaming".

Short sequences, it moans in pain, then it pleads, then it goes incoherent and screams "louder" (longer sequences).

If you don't like it, fair enough. But it's not weird for weirdness sake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback. I'll think about it. It kind of reads how I wanted it to read right now though.
I agree I may have gone overboard with the number designations.

(I'll just savor the few seconds where I'm actually at +3, right now.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just keep the edit window open before you start copy pasting. It will automatically lock the page without having to actually post anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall, the blocks of random text generated by the SCP felt unnecessary. Can you explain the significance of certain characters appearing more or less frequently, or is at supposed to just be another anomalous trait?

The word snippets felt more like part of a creepypasta than an SCP article. Overall, I don't particularly like the idea. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, tenses fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't realize it would be seen as soon as I did it, I just didn't want to fight someone else over the same number. I can see how it would be annoying to get an update and then nothing in it. Won't happen again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Speaking from a computer science perspective, this article has way too much information where none is needed and not enough where more is warranted. Is it the program that generates this output or the computer? Don't you think that would have been one of the first things we tested?

There's also way too much clutter involved; the weird titles for personnel as well as the excessive numeric designations make it hard to read. The excessive use of collapsibles in strange places also makes it hard to read from end to end and therefore hard to stay engaged. Finally, it's just simply not that interesting of an idea; at heart it's just an anomalous computer program that says spooky things then makes you go into a seizure.

Unfortunately I hold a much higher standard for computer-based SCPs and I have downvoted this as it does not meet said standards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's just one of my pet peeves. I really don't like it when I open up an article just to see what amounts to "Oh, I'm reserving this spot until I can finish my article."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dig it. My only suggestion would be to move the description of the computer into the "containment" section. I usually hit the "description" section hoping for the "reveal" of what SCP would actually need the containment described - having to go through another 2 paragraphs of backstory was like missing a step while walking down stairs.

Edit: also, you've got mixed tenses in the Incident Report.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did manage to get 2 downvotes in those 3 minutes, so apparently it was a bad idea…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorry…you caught me in the 3 minutes where I found an empty number, and started to put my article in :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it's being put together out from a sandbox, the thing was fully written in advance

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't remember if this is a rule or not, but it is very bad form to take up an article page and not even put anything on it. You either have an article ready to post or you don't.

Downvote for now till I see some actual content.
